summary:
The Dadiwan culture, named after a site in Gansu province of northwestern China and dated from approximately 6500 to 5000 BCE, was an early Neolithic culture of the upper Wei River valley — a westerly extension of the North China Plain agricultural zone. The Dadiwan site itself was excavated by Chinese archaeologists beginning in the 1970s and revealed an exceptionally long occupation sequence spanning several prehistoric cultural phases, with the earliest Neolithic levels among the oldest yet identified in northwestern China.

Dadiwan communities cultivated millet and kept domesticated pigs, and their material culture includes simple hand-built pottery with impressed and incised decorations. The site's long stratigraphic sequence makes it an important reference point for the cultural chronology of prehistoric northwestern China. In later periods (post-5000 BCE), the Dadiwan site shows evidence of more elaborate social organization, including a large public building interpreted as a communal hall — one of the earliest examples of large-scale communal architecture in prehistoric China.

The Dadiwan culture demonstrates the westward extent of the early millet-farming Neolithic across the Loess Plateau of China, and contributes to understanding how farming communities spread and adapted across the diverse environments of inland northwestern China during the early Neolithic period. Its connections to the subsequent Yangshao tradition link it to the mainstream of North Chinese Neolithic development.
